response: solution The erroneous code likely fails because it does not properly handle cases where duplicate values exceed half the array length, causing adjacent equal elements even when a valid wiggle arrangement exists. solution To fix this, we must sort the array and then interleave elements from the second half with the first half in reverse order to separate duplicates. solution This approach ensures that equal values are spaced apart, satisfying the condition that adjacent elements are not equal whenever possible. solution For the follow-up question, an O(n) solution without full sorting can be achieved using the QuickSelect algorithm to find the median and then partitioning the array around it. solution This median-based partitioning allows us to place smaller elements at even indices and larger elements at odd indices while maintaining the wiggle property. solution The method works for both positive and negative integers because the comparison logic relies only on relative ordering, not on the sign of the numbers. solution The final implementation guarantees that the array is reordered to meet the strict inequality constraints where feasible.
